figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: Study design and cohort overview. Adult human testicular samples were collected from fertile controls and patients with clinically defined azoospermia subtypes, including obstructive azoospermia (OA), AZFc microdeletion (AZFc_Del), Klinefelter syndrome (KS), idiopathic non-obstructive azoospermia with spermatogenic arrest (iNOA_B), and idiopathic Sertoli-cell-only syndrome (iNOA_S).
availability_note: No direct panel-prefixed source table found; included README/mapping files and figure-level source-data directory.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: UMAP embedding of the integrated single-cell transcriptomic atlas colored by clinical diagnosis, showing shared embedding of control and azoospermia samples after cross-donor harmonization.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: UMAP embedding colored by annotated cell type, resolving the major germline and somatic compartments of the adult human testis. Germline populations include spermatogonial stem/progenitor cells, spermatogonia, primary spermatocytes, round spermatids, elongated spermatids, and sperm. Somatic populations include Sertoli cells, Leydig cells, endothelial cells, peritubular myoid cells, myeloid cells, and lymphoid/immune populations.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: Feature plots showing normalized expression of canonical marker genes used for cell-type annotation. Representative markers include Sertoli-cell genes, endothelial-cell genes, Leydig-cell genes, immune-lineage genes, myoid-cell genes, and stage-specific germline markers spanning spermatogonial, meiotic, and post-meiotic states.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: Cell-type composition across clinical diagnoses, shown as stacked proportions of annotated populations. Controls and OA retain broad germline representation, whereas non-obstructive etiologies show etiology-dependent germline depletion or developmental arrest with relative enrichment of somatic compartments.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: Donor-level summary of retained cell numbers and compartmental composition after quality control, stratified by clinical group and germline versus somatic identity.

figure_title: A harmonized single-cell atlas resolves the cellular architecture of human testes across azoospermia etiologies.
panel_legend: Violin plots summarizing normalized expression of representative lineage and stage markers across annotated cell populations. Marker distributions support robust assignment of Sertoli, Leydig, endothelial, myoid, immune, spermatogonial, meiotic, and post-meiotic compartments. Color scales and group labels are as indicated in the panels.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: UMAP embedding of re-clustered germline cells, resolving a continuous spermatogenic trajectory from spermatogonial stem/progenitor cells and differentiating spermatogonia through early and late primary spermatocytes to round spermatids, elongated spermatids, and sperm.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Diagnosis-stratified distribution of germline states along the refined germline manifold. Early germline states remain partially shared across groups, whereas disease-associated depletion and redistribution become most prominent around late primary spermatocytes and downstream post-meiotic compartments.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Dot plot of canonical germline developmental markers used to anchor stage annotation. Dot size represents the fraction of cells expressing each gene, and color indicates scaled average expression. Meiotic markers identify primary spermatocytes, whereas transition-protein and protamine-family genes define post-meiotic spermatid and sperm states.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Pseudotime reconstruction of the germline developmental continuum. Cells are ordered from spermatogonial states through meiotic and post-meiotic stages, enabling stage-resolved comparison of control and disease trajectories.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Local group-mixing entropy calculated along germline pseudotime. Lower entropy indicates stronger separation between control and disease groups. The entropy trough identifies the late primary spermatocyte window as the earliest robust point of disease-control divergence.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Donor-resolved paired analysis of local group-mixing entropy in early versus late primary spermatocytes. Each line represents one donor, and line width reflects the number of informative cells contributing to the paired comparison. A predominantly negative late-minus-early entropy shift indicates increased divergence upon progression into late meiosis.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Gene set enrichment analysis of Hallmark oxidative phosphorylation in late primary spermatocytes comparing disease groups with controls. Disease-associated attenuation of oxidative phosphorylation indicates impaired mitochondrial energetic programs at the meiotic divergence window.

figure_title: Germline developmental divergence from controls emerges at late primary spermatocytes and propagates into post-meiotic stress programs.
panel_legend: Gene set enrichment analysis of Hallmark apoptosis in round spermatids comparing disease groups with controls. Increased apoptotic signaling in downstream post-meiotic cells indicates propagation of late-meiotic dysfunction into spermatid attrition. NES and adjusted P values are shown in the panels where applicable.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: UMAP embedding of the re-clustered somatic compartment colored by major somatic lineage, including Sertoli cells, Leydig cells, immune cells, peritubular myoid cells, and endothelial cells.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Integrated somatic niche-driver priority score ranking major somatic lineages according to remodeling burden, pathway perturbation, germline association, and niche-module involvement. Leydig cells rank highest, followed by Sertoli cells, nominating the interstitial endocrine axis and tubular support axis as the dominant somatic components associated with germline failure.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Disease-associated pathway enrichment in Leydig cells and Sertoli cells across azoospermia etiologies. Dot color indicates normalized enrichment score, and dot size indicates enrichment significance. Leydig-cell pathways include cholesterol homeostasis, fatty-acid metabolism, oxidative phosphorylation, TNFα/NF-κB signaling, extracellular-matrix/adhesion programs, hypoxia, and apoptosis. Sertoli-cell pathways include ECM/adhesion, TNFα/NF-κB signaling, interferon response, apoptosis, p53 stress, glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Donor-level heatmap of integrated somatic niche scores, including Sertoli maturity, BTB integrity, Sertoli stress, Sertoli SASP-like activity, Leydig maturity, and Leydig immaturity. Donors are ordered along the niche-collapse axis from a preserved state to a collapsed state. Values are z-scored across donors.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Core Leydig–Sertoli–germline association map summarizing donor-level correlations among Leydig maturity/immaturity, Sertoli maturity, BTB integrity, germline maturity, and latest germline stage detected. Red edges indicate positive correlations, blue edges indicate negative correlations, and line width reflects the absolute Spearman correlation coefficient.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Principal-component embedding of donors based on integrated niche scores. PC1 represents the dominant niche-collapse axis, separating donors with mature, barrier-intact somatic niches from donors with stress-enriched and Leydig-immature niche states.

figure_title: Somatic niche prioritization identifies coordinated Leydig–Sertoli collapse linked to germline outcome.
panel_legend: Association between the niche-collapse axis and germline outcomes. Higher niche-collapse scores correlate with lower germline maturity index and earlier latest germline stage recovered per donor. Spearman correlation coefficients and P values are shown in the panels.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Experimental design of the EDS-induced Leydig-cell ablation and testosterone-rescue model. Mice were assigned to vehicle, EDS, or EDS plus testosterone replacement groups. Leydig-cell ablation was assessed at day 3 using CYP11A1, Sertoli dysfunction and blood–testis barrier (BTB) damage were assessed at day 7 using AMH and CLDN11, and late spermatocyte/round spermatid impairment was assessed at day 14 using SYCP3.
availability_note: Experimental-design schematic; animal metadata table was not located in the workspace.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Representative immunofluorescence staining of CYP11A1, WT1/DHH, CYP11A1/GAS1, AMH, CLDN11, and SYCP3 in testes from vehicle, EDS, and EDS+T groups. CYP11A1 marks Leydig cells, WT1 marks Sertoli cells, DHH represents Sertoli-side Hedgehog ligand signal, GAS1 represents Leydig-side Hedgehog receptor/co-receptor signal, AMH indicates Sertoli regression or dysfunction, CLDN11 marks BTB-associated tight junctions, and SYCP3 marks meiotic spermatocytes. Nuclei were counterstained with DAPI. Scale bars are indicated in the images.
availability_note: Representative IF panel; raw microscopy files/channel metadata were not located. Quantified measures should be supported by panel c/e source data.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Quantification of CYP11A1-positive interstitial cells across treatment groups. EDS markedly reduced CYP11A1-positive Leydig cells, confirming efficient Leydig-cell ablation. Testosterone replacement did not restore CYP11A1-positive Leydig-cell abundance to vehicle levels.
availability_note: CYP11A1-positive cell quantification requires animal/sample-level cell-count or density data; current workspace has a fill-in template only.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Serum testosterone levels across vehicle, EDS, and EDS+T groups. EDS depleted circulating testosterone, whereas testosterone replacement restored serum testosterone to near-vehicle levels, separating systemic androgen rescue from structural recovery of the Leydig-cell compartment.
availability_note: Serum testosterone quantification requires hormone assay output with animal IDs, group, value, unit and batch; current workspace has a fill-in template only.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Integrated quantification summary of local niche and germline readouts. Values for DHH, GAS1, AMH, CLDN11, and SYCP3 were normalized to the vehicle mean for each metric. DHH and GAS1 summarize the Sertoli–Leydig Hedgehog axis, AMH and CLDN11 summarize Sertoli/BTB integrity, and SYCP3 summarizes meiotic germ-cell support. Small points represent individual animals or biological replicates, and large points indicate group means. The dashed vertical line denotes the vehicle-normalized baseline. V–E, Vehicle versus EDS; V–T, Vehicle versus EDS+T; E–T, EDS versus EDS+T. Statistical significance was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple-comparison test.
availability_note: Integrated DHH/GAS1/AMH/CLDN11/SYCP3 quantification requires ROI/image-level fluorescence, CLDN11 continuity and SYCP3 measurements; current workspace has a fill-in template only.

figure_title: Leydig-cell ablation induces persistent local niche dysfunction despite systemic testosterone rescue.
panel_legend: Working model summarizing the functional consequence of Leydig-cell ablation. EDS induces Leydig-cell loss and androgen decline, followed by reduced Leydig-side Hedgehog receptor competence, Sertoli dysfunction, CLDN11 disorganization, and late spermatocyte/round spermatid impairment. Testosterone replacement restores systemic androgen levels and partially improves downstream Sertoli/BTB and germline phenotypes, but it does not restore Leydig-cell abundance or fully recover local Sertoli–Leydig niche communication. Bars represent mean ± SEM; dots indicate individual animals or biological replicates. ns, not significant; *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001; ****P < 0.0001. EDS, ethane dimethanesulfonate; EDS+T, EDS plus testosterone replacement; LC, Leydig cell; BTB, blood–testis barrier; DHH, Desert Hedgehog; T, testosterone; late-SPC, late primary spermatocyte.
availability_note: Mechanistic summary model; no numeric source data expected, but claims should cite the raw measurements for panels c-e.

figure_title: Marker-based annotation and functional enrichment support major germline and somatic lineage definitions in the integrated human testis atlas.
panel_legend: UMAP feature plots showing normalized expression of representative genes used to annotate major testicular lineages and germline developmental stages. Sertoli cells are marked by BEX1 and WT1; endothelial cells by PECAM1 and CDH5; Leydig cells by INSL3 and IGF1; myeloid cells by CD163 and CD14; peritubular myoid cells by FAM129A and ACTA2; SSC/progenitor states by UTF1 and PIWIL4; spermatogonia by MKI67 and STRA8; early primary spermatocytes by DMC1 and BRCA1; late primary spermatocytes by PSMC3IP and AURKA; and spermatids by PRM2 and SPEM1. Color intensity indicates normalized expression.

figure_title: Marker-based annotation and functional enrichment support major germline and somatic lineage definitions in the integrated human testis atlas.
panel_legend: Heatmap of selected marker genes across annotated cell populations, together with Gene Ontology enrichment of marker-gene sets. The enrichment terms support lineage identity and biological function, including spermatid differentiation, angiogenesis/endothelial programs, steroid-hormone response, lymphocyte activation, myeloid immune signaling, actin cytoskeleton organization, meiotic cell cycle, RNA splicing, Sertoli-cell metabolic support, sperm motility, fertilization, and cilium movement. Dot size indicates gene ratio, and dot color indicates −log10(P value).

figure_title: Diagnosis-stratified germline marker expression and pathway remodeling validate the late-meiotic divergence window.
panel_legend: Diagnosis-stratified feature plots of canonical meiotic and post-meiotic markers across the re-clustered germline embedding. SYCP3 and PIWIL1 localize to spermatocyte compartments, whereas TNP1 and PRM2 mark post-meiotic spermatid/sperm states. Severe non-obstructive disease shows attenuation or loss of downstream germline programs.

figure_title: Diagnosis-stratified germline marker expression and pathway remodeling validate the late-meiotic divergence window.
panel_legend: Dot plot summarizing expression of SYCP3, PIWIL1, TNP1, and PRM2 across diagnostic groups. Dot size indicates percent expressed, and color indicates average expression.

figure_title: Diagnosis-stratified germline marker expression and pathway remodeling validate the late-meiotic divergence window.
panel_legend: Hallmark pathway enrichment in late primary spermatocytes comparing each disease group with controls. Dot color indicates normalized enrichment score, and dot size indicates −log10(FDR). Disease-associated changes include attenuation of oxidative phosphorylation and remodeling of hypoxia, DNA repair, ROS, cholesterol homeostasis, glycolysis, and proliferative programs.

figure_title: Diagnosis-stratified germline marker expression and pathway remodeling validate the late-meiotic divergence window.
panel_legend: Hallmark pathway enrichment in round spermatids comparing disease groups with controls. Post-meiotic disease-associated signatures include enrichment of apoptosis, interferon responses, inflammatory signaling, TNFα/NF-κB signaling, p53 pathway, epithelial–mesenchymal transition, and related stress programs.

figure_title: Contextual somatic remodeling supports a coordinated Leydig–Sertoli–germline niche-collapse program.
panel_legend: Hallmark pathway enrichment analysis across contextual somatic lineages, including immune cells, myoid cells, and endothelial cells. Disease-associated remodeling includes TNFα/NF-κB signaling, oxidative phosphorylation, myogenesis, spermatogenesis-associated signatures, epithelial–mesenchymal transition, p53 pathway, hypoxia, and stress-response programs. Dot color indicates normalized enrichment score, and dot size indicates −log10(FDR).

figure_title: Contextual somatic remodeling supports a coordinated Leydig–Sertoli–germline niche-collapse program.
panel_legend: Donor-level correlation matrix linking core Sertoli, Leydig, and germline niche scores. Sertoli maturity and BTB integrity positively correlate with Leydig maturity and germline outcome, whereas Leydig immaturity is negatively associated with Sertoli support features and germline maturity. Values indicate Spearman correlation coefficients; significance is marked in the matrix.

figure_title: Contextual somatic remodeling supports a coordinated Leydig–Sertoli–germline niche-collapse program.
panel_legend: Group-level summary heatmap of core Leydig–Sertoli niche scores and germline outcomes across diagnoses. Values are row-wise z-scored means, highlighting preserved niche features in controls/OA and collapsed niche features in severe non-obstructive etiologies.

figure_title: State-resolved Sertoli-cell transcriptional and pathway remodeling reveals disease-associated stress, inflammatory, junctional, and metabolic programs.
panel_legend: Pseudotime-ordered heatmap of representative Sertoli-cell genes across Stage_a, Stage_b, and Stage_c. Genes are grouped into modules corresponding to early/reactive, intermediate, and mature-supportive Sertoli states. Expression values are scaled by gene.

figure_title: State-resolved Sertoli-cell transcriptional and pathway remodeling reveals disease-associated stress, inflammatory, junctional, and metabolic programs.
panel_legend: Heatmap of stage-enriched Sertoli regulon activity across Stage_a–c. Early/reactive states show enrichment of stress-associated regulons, including AP-1-related programs, whereas Stage_c shows distinct regulatory features associated with mature Sertoli identity.

figure_title: State-resolved Sertoli-cell transcriptional and pathway remodeling reveals disease-associated stress, inflammatory, junctional, and metabolic programs.
panel_legend: Pathway enrichment in Stage_a immature-like Sertoli cells comparing disease groups with controls. Enriched pathways include chemokine signaling, endocytosis, focal adhesion, MAPK signaling, actin cytoskeleton regulation, Wnt signaling, insulin signaling, tight junction, oxidative phosphorylation, and spliceosome. Dot color indicates NES, and dot size indicates −log10(FDR).

figure_title: State-resolved Sertoli-cell transcriptional and pathway remodeling reveals disease-associated stress, inflammatory, junctional, and metabolic programs.
panel_legend: Pathway enrichment in Stage_c mature Sertoli cells comparing disease groups with controls. Disease-associated changes involve apoptosis, cell adhesion molecules, chemokine signaling, endocytosis, insulin signaling, MAPK signaling, oxidative phosphorylation, actin cytoskeleton regulation, spliceosome, and Wnt signaling, indicating that even Sertoli cells retaining mature identity undergo disease-associated remodeling.

figure_title: Leydig-cell pathway scores and GO-program matrices reveal reciprocal activation of interstitial remodeling and repression of steroidogenic metabolism.
panel_legend: Violin plots showing pathway/activity scores across Leydig states, including luteinizing hormone score, steroid metabolism score, and glucocorticoid receptor pathway score. Leydig Stage_c shows increased luteinizing hormone and steroid metabolism scores, whereas glucocorticoid receptor pathway activity is higher in earlier Leydig states. Statistical significance is indicated in the panels.

figure_title: Leydig-cell pathway scores and GO-program matrices reveal reciprocal activation of interstitial remodeling and repression of steroidogenic metabolism.
panel_legend: High-level GO-program matrix summarizing Leydig-cell programs upregulated in disease relative to controls across AZFc_Del, iNOA_B, iNOA_S, and KS. Upregulated programs include collagen/ECM remodeling, adhesion/cell-substrate junction, vesicle/secretory lumen, interstitial/tissue development, actin/contractile cytoskeleton, growth-factor/TGF-β receptor axes, translational/transcriptional co-regulation, and oxidative-stress response.

figure_title: Leydig-cell pathway scores and GO-program matrices reveal reciprocal activation of interstitial remodeling and repression of steroidogenic metabolism.
panel_legend: High-level GO-program matrix summarizing Leydig-cell programs downregulated in disease relative to controls. Downregulated programs converge on steroid biosynthesis, steroid/cholesterol metabolism, mitochondrial compartments, oxidative phosphorylation/electron transport, respiration/ATP generation, fatty-acid/lipid metabolism, vesicle/lysosomal lumen, proteostasis/chaperone activity, adhesion/junction, and oxidoreductase/antioxidant functions.

figure_title: Metabolic pathway activity and reaction-level flux inference support stage-specific energetic remodeling of spermatogenesis.
panel_legend: Heatmap of scMetabolism-derived KEGG metabolic pathway activity scores across germline stages, including SSCs, spermatogonia, early primary spermatocytes, late primary spermatocytes, round spermatids, elongated spermatids, and sperm. Pathways include glutathione metabolism, glycolysis/gluconeogenesis, pyruvate metabolism, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, amino-acid metabolism, lipid metabolism, steroid biosynthesis, and fatty-acid degradation.
availability_note: available; Numeric matrix and top30 order are available

figure_title: Metabolic pathway activity and reaction-level flux inference support stage-specific energetic remodeling of spermatogenesis.
panel_legend: scFEA-inferred reaction-level metabolic flux across germline developmental stages. Flux modules related to glycolysis, pyruvate-to-lactate conversion, acetyl-CoA/TCA entry, citrate cycle reactions, glutathione metabolism, amino-acid metabolism, and mitochondrial reactions show coordinated changes around the late primary spermatocyte and post-meiotic transition.

figure_title: Metabolic pathway activity and reaction-level flux inference support stage-specific energetic remodeling of spermatogenesis.
panel_legend: Diagnosis- and cell-type-resolved metabolic flux deviations across selected reaction modules. Heatmap values represent standardized flux changes across cell types and clinical groups, highlighting disease-associated rewiring of fatty-acid metabolism, cholesterol/steroid-related reactions, nucleotide metabolism, glycolysis, TCA-cycle reactions, and amino-acid metabolism.

figure_title: Obstructive azoospermia preserves testicular architecture but remodels post-meiotic germ-cell and somatic niche programs.
panel_legend: Gene Ontology enrichment analysis of OA-associated Sertoli-cell differentially expressed genes. Downregulated programs include supramolecular fiber organization, actin filament organization, actin cytoskeleton organization, actin filament-based process, and cell-substrate adhesion, whereas upregulated programs include cellular respiration, ATP metabolic process, aerobic respiration, oxidative phosphorylation, and aerobic electron transport chain.
availability_note: available; direct per-cell input table (fig8h.csv) and raw violin PDFs are available

figure_title: Obstructive azoospermia preserves testicular architecture but remodels post-meiotic germ-cell and somatic niche programs.
panel_legend: Gene Ontology enrichment analysis of OA-associated Leydig-cell differentially expressed genes. Downregulated programs include protein folding, response to oxidative stress, cellular oxidant detoxification, RNA splicing, and response to interleukin-7, whereas upregulated programs include aerobic respiration, cellular respiration, electron transport chain, respiratory electron transport chain, and oxidative phosphorylation. Together, these analyses indicate that OA preserves spermatogenesis but represents an obstruction-associated adaptive state rather than a molecularly neutral control.
